title: Recruitment Violation and Related Cases - Legal Assistance and Enforcement Measures
content:
Section 1 defines Illegal Recruitment as acts of canvassing, enlisting, contracting, transporting, utilizing, hiring, or procuring workers for employment abroad by non-licensees or non-holders of authority, including offering or promising employment abroad for a fee to two or more persons. It also lists acts constituting illegal recruitment whether or not committed by licensees, such as charging fees from applicant seafarers, publishing false information, misrepresentation to secure licenses, inducing employed seafarers to quit without valid cause, obstructing inspections, failure to submit required reports, altering approved contracts without approval, conflicts of interest with travel agencies, withholding travel documents for unauthorized fees, failure to deploy without valid reasons, and failure to reimburse expenses when deployment does not occur without fault of the seafarer.

Section 2 states that administrative actions against licensees or holders of authority may proceed independently of criminal actions.

Section 3 mandates the Administration to adopt policies and programs to eradicate illegal recruitment, including victim assistance, processing suspected recruiters, surveillance operations, and information campaigns, coordinating with other entities as needed.

Section 4 requires the Administration to provide free legal assistance to victims of illegal recruitment, including legal advice, complaint preparation, criminal action institution, and counseling during investigations and hearings.

Section 5 allows victims to file written, sworn complaints with the Administration or regional offices.

Section 6 provides for surveillance upon complaints alleging ongoing illegal recruitment, recommending closure orders if confirmed, and forwarding cases for criminal action if warranted.

Section 7 authorizes the Administrator or designated officials to conduct surveillance on alleged illegal recruitment and submit reports within two days.

Section 8 empowers the Secretary, Administrator, or Regional Director to conduct ex-parte preliminary examinations and issue closure orders if illegal recruitment activities pose danger or exploitation, including recommending revocation of local business licenses.

Section 9 mandates serving closure orders, sealing establishments, posting notices, and requesting law enforcement assistance as needed.

Section 10 requires submission of an under-oath report on closure order implementation within two days.

Section 11 allows authorized officials or aggrieved persons to initiate criminal actions and requires filing complaints with supporting documents.

Section 12 permits motions to lift closure orders within ten calendar days, requiring stated grounds and supporting documents; non-conforming motions are denied.

Section 13 specifies who may file motions to lift closure orders: building owners or representatives, building administrators or representatives, persons/entities subject to closure or their representatives, and other legitimate occupants with distinct operations.

Section 14 lists grounds for lifting or reopening: office not subject to closure, lease cancellation with affidavit of undertaking not to lease for recruitment without license, shared office with uninvolved parties, or other valid grounds. Lifting does not preclude criminal complaints.

Section 15 allows appeal of denial of motion to lift to the Secretary within ten calendar days.

Section 16 states that re-opened offices confirmed to engage in illegal recruitment may be re-closed with no further motions to lift allowed.
